coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation on the performance of MXene/C-dot hybrid nanofluid-based photovoltaic/thermal system: An Energy, Exergy, and Enviro-Economic analysis

coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids (NFs) with exceptional heat transfer properties are increasingly being adopted in solar collectors as heat transfer fluid (HTF). Hybrid photovoltaic thermal systems are becoming more popular because they increase overall efficiency by producing both high- and low-grade electricity. This research focuses on the application of thermally efficient NFs for enhancing the thermal and electrical output of a photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) system. Indoor experimental investigation was conducted on the PV/T system to find the thermally efficient HTF. Carbon quantum dot (C-dot), MXene and MXene/C-dot hybrid NFs were selected for experimentation. Thermo-economic factors (thermal conductivity, volumetric heat capacity, thermal property degradation rate, and cost) were used to optimise the NFs' concentration. Optimized C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NFs were used for experimentation in the PV/T system. Energy, exergy, and enviro-economic analysis was conducted on the PV/T system with each HTFs. The results show that the thermal efficiency of PV/T was enhanced by 5.39, 3.87, and 9.45 % with C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs, respectively. Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) analysis was performed on the collector from the generated experimental data. The analysis states that at zero heat loss conditions, the thermal efficiency of the PV/T was enhanced by 5.4 % with the hybrid NF. The maximum CO2 emission from PV/T with C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NFs, were calculated to be around 2.024, 1.979, and 1.931 kgCO2/day, respectively. Emission cost reduction analysis states that the hybrid NF achieved the highest reduction of 0.02 $/day which was a considerable decrement of 13.5 % over the water-based PV/T system.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids (NFs) with exceptional heat transfer properties are increasingly being adopted in solar collectors as heat transfer fluid (HTF). Hybrid photovoltaic thermal systems are becoming more popular because they increase overall efficiency by producing both high- and low-grade electricity. This research focuses on the application of thermally efficient NFs for enhancing the thermal and electrical output of a photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) system. Indoor experimental investigation was conducted on the PV/T system to find the thermally efficient HTF. Carbon quantum dot (C-dot), MXene and MXene/C-dot hybrid NFs were selected for experimentation. Thermo-economic factors (thermal conductivity, volumetric heat capacity, thermal property degradation rate, and cost) were used to optimise the NFs' concentration. Optimized C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NFs were used for experimentation in the PV/T system. Energy, exergy, and enviro-economic analysis was conducted on the PV/T system with each HTFs. The results show that the thermal efficiency of PV/T was enhanced by 5.39, 3.87, and 9.45 % with C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs, respectively. Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) analysis was performed on the collector from the generated experimental data. The analysis states that at zero heat loss conditions, the thermal efficiency of the PV/T was enhanced by 5.4 % with the hybrid NF. The maximum CO2 emission from PV/T with C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NFs, were calculated to be around 2.024, 1.979, and 1.931 kgCO2/day, respectively. Emission cost reduction analysis states that the hybrid NF achieved the highest reduction of 0.02 $/day which was a considerable decrement of 13.5 % over the water-based PV/T system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nomenclature

ce:section.ce:para: Exploitation of fossil fuels to meet growing energy demands has resulted in excessive carbon emissions which has resulted in numerous environmental impacts including global warming, rising sea level, destruction of ecosystem, and health hazards [1,2]. Research in the renewable energy sector has helped to mitigate the rise in pollution and excessive dependence on conventional resources. Solar energy is the most abundant, easily accessible, and flexible renewable energy source. Solar energy could be harnessed using various energy conversion methods to meet a variety of applications. Photovoltaic [3], photothermal [4], photochemical [5], and photobiological [6] energy conversion systems have already been developed. As a large proportion of electricity production in the future will depend on photovoltaics (PV), the research on the efficiency enhancement of PV systems is accelerated. But, the maximum achievable energy conversion efficiency of PV is around 30 % [7]. PV efficiency depreciates by 0.5 % with a rise in cell temperature by one degree [8]. An increase in temperature increases carrier recombination rates and results in lower power generation [9,10]. To counteract the drawbacks, various methods for cooling the PV panel have been developed [11]. Hybrid PV/T is one such system that reduces the PV surface temperature. A hybrid PV/T collector is an energy conversion device that typically consists of a PV module and a heat exchanger attached to the PV panel. The heat exchanger comprises an absorber plate which might be attached to the rear end of the PV panel, and an HTF which takes away the heat from the absorber plate. The portion of thermal energy received by the PV surface, after photovoltaic conversion, gets transferred to the absorber plate and finally reaches the HTF by conduction and convection heat transfer [12].

Commonly used liquid HTFs include deionized water (DI), ethylene glycol (EG), propylene glycol (PEG), and thermal oils (Duratherm S, Syltherm 800) [13]. Research on conventional fluid-based PV/T has been explored largely in the past. Atiz et al. [14] investigated the influence of water flow rate on the electricity production rate of a PV/T. The study shows that the electricity production increased with an increase in flow rate and the highest electrical efficiency was achieved at a flow rate of 50 gs−1. Azad and Parvin [15] conducted a numerical study on the thermal and electrical performance of water-based PV/T collectors at three different flow regimes. The study reveals that the system performed better at transition and laminar flow, and the electrical efficiency was observed to decrease at creeping flow conditions. The drawback faced by conventional HTF includes poor thermophysical properties and lack of characteristics tunability to suit a specific application.

NFs exhibit exceptional thermal transport in comparison to conventional HTFs [16]. NFs are developed by dispersing the nanomaterials in the base fluid. Better thermal conductivity, lower viscosity, high dispersion stability, and lower cost are a few desirable characteristics of an HTF. Studies have reported that PV/T performance could be enhanced with NFs [17]. Researchers have studied the thermal and electrical performance of PV/T with a variety of conventional NFs like metal/metal oxide [18] and carbon-based NFs [19]. Gelis et al. [20] experimented on SiO2/water-based PV/T and optimized the operating parameters using response surface methodology. The most influencing parameter on the system efficiency was reported as irradiation, flow rate and NF concentrations, respectively. Research conducted by Venkatesh et al. [21] shows that the usage of water-based graphene NF could enhance PV/T efficiency by 13 %. Chaichan et al. [22] investigated the effect of NF synthesis parameters, including sonication time, the surfactant used, and MWCNT nanoparticle concentration, on the PV/T cooling performance. Thermal and electrical performance was found to be augmented by 20 and 44 %, respectively. Ahmadlou et al. [23] investigated the performance of a PV/T and thermoelectric (TE) system with single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotube (CNT) NFs. Results show that the electrical efficiency and power produced by the system are enhanced with the application of NFs. In addition to the conventional NFs, a few novel NFs including 2-dimensional nanomaterials and quantum dots were also developed recently. Samylingam et al. [17] numerically studied the performance augmentation of hybrid PV/T system with novel MXene/Olein-palm-oil NFs. The study shows that MXene NF based PV/T achieved enhancement in thermal and electrical efficiencies by around 11.2 and 13.8 %, respectively, over that with conventional alumina NF. Numerical investigation on MXene/water-based PV/T system showed that usage of NFs made a significant improvement in thermal efficiency by around 17 % over base fluid [24]. Investigation on thermo-hydraulic performance and entropy generation rate of MXene/Soyabean oil-based evacuated tube solar collector was conducted by Awis et al. [25]. The study reveals that MXene nanofluid of 0.075 wt% demonstrates better thermal efficiency and lower entropy generation.

C-dot NF was found to have recently been adopted in heat transfer systems due to its optical and thermophysical characteristics [26-28]. Xiao et al. [29] were the first to adopt a C-dot NF as an optical liquid filter in a spectral splitting PV/T system due to its spectral-specific optical absorptivity. The results indicate that the C-dot dispersed in PEG is an efficient working fluid and the system achieved a merit function as high as 1.904. Despite better thermophysical properties, C-dot has not been employed as a heat transfer fluid in PV/T. 2-D MXene nanosheets were also getting research focus due to their significant heat transfer properties. Also, MXene nanoparticle dispersion was found to ameliorate the thermal conductivity of base fluid significantly [30]. The major drawback faced by MXene nanoparticles is their dispersion stability. Studies have shown that hybridizing two different materials could generate a hybrid nanomaterial with complementary characteristics of its components [31,32]. Hybrid nanomaterial dispersions or hybrid NFs are recently being adopted in heat transfer applications.

An experimental study by Siddiqui et al. [32] proved that hybrid Cu–Al2O3 NF exhibited synergistic thermal conductivity and stability enhancement, which were the characteristic properties of individual NFs. In our recent research work, a novel MXene/C-dot hybrid NF was developed, and thermo-optical characterization was conducted. The study shows that C-dot, MXene, and MXene/C-dot NFs exhibited thermal conductivity enhancement of 33.2, 50, and 42.2 %, respectively, over base fluid. The hybrid NF was observed to show synergistic stability enhancement and high thermal conductivity characteristics of C-dot and MXene NFs, respectively. A corrosion study conducted on DI, C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs shows that MXene/C-dot developed the highest corrosion resistance on copper surfaces, which indicates the feasibility for long-term usage in heat transfer systems [33]. Water-based CuO–Fe hybrid NF was used as HTF in a photovoltaic/thermal system by Gharapetian et al. [34]. The study shows that adoption of hybrid NF enhanced the PV power production by around 66 % over that with water cooling. In comparison to other conventional fluids, the experimental studies on MXene and C-dot NFs’ application as an HTF in PV/T are lesser [35,36].

The literature review reveals that C-dot and MXene nanoparticle dispersions significantly enhance the thermophysical properties of the conventional base fluid. Moreover, investigations on the usage of C-dot, and MXene NF as HTF in PV/T systems were rarely explored. Hybrid MXene/C-dot NF was found to be exhibiting improved thermophysical properties demanded for an HTF. To the best of the authors' knowledge, experimental research on MXene, C-dot and MXene/C-dot-based PV/T systems has not been published to date. Hence, this study focuses on the performance evaluation of a hybrid PV/T system with C-dot, MXene, and MXene/C-dot hybrid NF. Study evaluates the performance enhancement of PV/T achieved with NFs over base fluid. The concentration of the NF was optimized based on thermo-economic factors, to reduce the experimentation cost and time. A novel optimization methodology was adopted for the present study to optimise the nanofluid concentration based on thermo-economic parameters. Energy and exergy analysis was performed on the PV/T system which was operated with optimized NFs. A comprehensive comparison was conducted based on the thermal and electrical performance of the system with NFs and base fluid. An HWB analysis was performed using the experimentally generated data. Finally, an enviro-economic analysis was carried out to evaluate the best HTF that could reduce the carbon emission and associated carbon emission cost. A comprehensive study on nanofluid concentration optimization, experimental investigation on PV/T, and analysis (energy, exergy, HWB, and enviro-economic analysis) has not been conducted to date.

High-quality research-grade ammonium hydrogen citrate dibasic (C6H14N2O7, ACS Reagent 98 % purity) was procured from Sigma Aldrich, to be used as the carbon source for C-dot synthesis. Multi-layered MXene nanosheets (Ti3C2-Tx, ACS Reagent, 99 % purity) were purchased from NanoChemazone (Canada) for the synthesis of the MXene NF. These raw materials were used for the synthesis of hybrid NF. The synthesis methodology adopted for C-dot, MXene, and MXene/C-dot hybrid NFs was explained in detail in our previous study [30,33]. DI water was used as the base fluid. A two-step method which involves dispersing the synthesized nanomaterials in the base fluid was adopted in this study for synthesizing the NFs. C-dot nanomaterial was prepared by microwave pyrolysis technique [30]. In this method, ammonium hydrogen citrate dibasic (2 g) which acts as the carbon source was dissolved in DI water (10 ml). The obtained clear solution was subjected to microwave treatment at 800 W for 6 min. The resulting product was dried in an air oven at 150 °C. The dried C-dot was weighed and dispersed in DI water to make NFs of the desired concentration. 2 g of raw material taken in the initial stage produced around 1.5 g of C-dot as the final output. MXene NF was prepared by directly dispersing the MXene in DI water using probe sonication. Modified microwave-assisted method as explained for C-dot was adopted for the preparation of hybrid nanoparticles [30,33]. In the preparation method, after dissolving ammonium hydrogen citrate in DI water, pure MXene was also added. MXene concentration in this step was selected based on the required concentration ratio of MXene and C-dot needed in the final hybrid nanoparticle. The mixture of carbon source and MXene was agitated using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature. Microwave treatment was applied to the resulting solution. The hybrid nanomaterial from the microwave treatment was dried in an oven to avoid moisture content. The collected nanoparticles were dispersed in the base fluid using the two-step method.

The PV/T system requires a significant amount of nanomaterials for experimentation. Hence, it is unwise to experiment on the collector with several concentrations of each NF, as it increases the wastage of material, energy, and experimentation time. Therefore, it is necessary to optimise the NF concentration on the basis of basic parameters including thermal conductivity, volumetric heat capacity, thermal conductivity degradation rate, and cost. This methodology helps to select the best NF concentration for each type of NF that has the highest potential to enhance the performance of the system. The thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity characteristics of the NFs were analysed and reported in our previous study [30]. The characteristics of the NFs at five different concentrations (0.01–0.2 wt%) were evaluated in the temperature range of 20–60 °C. The selected temperature range represents the normal operating range of a low-temperature PV/T system. The average thermal conductivity of each NF concentration, in selected temperature range, was considered in this optimization study. Similarly, the average value for the volumetric heat capacity of each NF, within this temperature range, was taken. The method of thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity characterization was detailed in our previous study [30]. Another predominant contributor adopted as a parameter in this optimization study is the degradation rate of thermal conductivity. The thermal degradation rate analysis was performed during a period of 14 days. The analysis is detailed in our previous study [33]. The price per litre of the nanofluid is the fourth optimization parameter. Two major criteria in terms of thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity values is initially defined. Average thermal conductivity value of 0.75 Wm−1K−1 and 20 % enhancement over DI water was considered as one of the minimum criteria for optimization. As the volumetric heat capacity value was decreasing with concentration, a minimum average volumetric heat capacity value of 3.6 MJm−3K−1 or maximum degradation of 5 % (over DI water) was considered as the second criteria for optimization. Values of thermal degradation rate and cost was monitored based on the trend in the variation of the values. Fig. 1 , and Fig. 2 show the variation of each parameter of C-dot and MXene, respectively, with concentration. As inferred from Fig. 1, the thermal conductivity of the C-dot increases with concentration. The minimum thermal conductivity criteria of around 0.75 Wm−1K−1 was achieved from 0.15 wt%. Also, it could be observed that the thermal conductivity degrades over time and reaches a value of 3.85 % for 0.2 wt%. The volumetric heat capacity was observed to decrease with the concentration from 3.89 to 3.57 MJm−3K−1. Volumetric heat capacity value was found to decrease by maximum of around 8.5 % for 0.2 wt% C-dot NF over that of DI water. At 0.2 wt% the minimum volumetric specific heat criteria of 3.6 MJm−3K−1 was not achieved due to degradation. The volumetric heat capacity decrease was around 6.8 % for 0.15 wt% NF. The cost of NF increased steadily with concentration. Cost of nanofluid has less significance on optimization as the cost of C-dot nanomaterial synthesis was lower. Thermal property degradation rate was found to be increasing significantly after 0.15 wt%. The optimum concentration was found to be 0.15 wt%, where the parameters have desirable values of 0.78 Wm−1K−1, 3.64 MJm−3K−1, 2.09 £/ltr, and 3.69 %.

Similarly, the optimum parameters for MXene were observed to be for the concentration of 0.1 wt% as shown in Fig. 2. Minimum thermal conductivity criteria was achieved by 0.1, 0.15 and 0.2 wt% MXene NF. Thermal degradation was observed to increase drastically with increase in concentration from 0.01 to 0.15 wt%. The degradation increased negligibly after 0.15 wt%. Cost is a significant parameter for optimization of MXene NF as the cost of MXene nanomaterial is relatively high compared to C-dot. Cost was found to be increasing significantly after 0.1 wt%. Therefore, the ideal concentration was determined to be 0.1 wt%, which is below the critical value (of 0.15 wt%). The characteristics at optimum concentration were 0.7628 Wm−1K−1, 3.771 MJm−3K−1, 17.45 £/ltr, and 6.91 %.

In the case of hybrid nanoparticles, the concentration mixing ratio of the raw material was also taken into consideration. Five different mixing ratios of C-dot and MXene in the ratios 5:1, 4:1, 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3, were considered for the synthesis. All the resulting hybrid nanoparticles of different compositions were dispersed in base fluid at the same concentration of 0.1 wt%. The properties of the MXene/C-dot NFs were compared as shown in Fig. 3 for selecting the optimum mixing ratio. The results show that hybrid NF with C-dot and MXene in the ratio 2:1 performs better in comparison to the other fluids. As inferred from Fig. 4 , the trend in variation of thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity is like other fluids. The minimum thermal conductivity criteria was achieved by 0.15 and 0.2 wt% MXene/C-dot NF. While the minimum required specific heat value of 3.6 MJm−3K−1 was not achieved by 0.2 wt%. The thermal degradation rate rises steeply with an increase in a concentration above 0.01 wt%. However, the degradation rate shows a negative slope from 0.1 to 0.15 wt%. Hence, 0.15 wt% was selected as the optimum concentration for the hybrid NF. The optimum concentration of the optimizing parameter values is 0.786 Wm−1K−1, 3.68 MJm−3K−1, 10.13 £/ltr, and 6.21 %.

Indoor experimentation was performed using an in-house solar simulator. The solar simulator consists of 7 rows of 5 metal halide lamps as shown in Fig. 5 . The solar radiation intensity could be adjusted along the rows and for each lamp using an associated dimmer setup. The radiation intensity was set to be almost uniform across the area of study by adjusting the intensity in each row. Picolog data acquisition unit was used to monitor the required temperatures during the experiment. DS1000-IV curve tracer was employed to plot the I–V curve of the PV/T module during the experiment at different experimental conditions. The iFM-2000 flow meter was used to measure the flow rate of the working fluid in lpm. An IR-imaging temperature gun was used to record the surface temperature of the PV/T module at different operating conditions. The distribution of simulated radiation over the PV/T aperture area at three irradiation levels (350, 500, and 650 W m-2) is shown in Fig. 6 . The incident radiation at the centre of each PV cell (of the PV/T) was calculated to find the average irradiation received by the PV/T surface. Irradiation at each PV cell is represented by a matrix as shown. The top right cell represents the PV cell close to the inlet and the bottom left indicates the cell close to the outlet. Fine tuning of the simulated radiation using the dimmer setup has resulted in the distribution as shown in the figure. However, minor, and unavoidable non-uniformity could be observed between the irradiation at the middle and upper/lower sections of the panel. The average solar radiation on the surface was calculated and used for analysis. The model's name, measuring range, and accuracy of the monitoring instruments are detailed in Table 1 .

The experimental setup consists of a hybrid PV/T collector being simulated by the indoor solar simulator. The schematic diagram of the experimental test rig is shown in Fig. 7 . As observed, the working fluid is circulated in the hydraulic loop with the help of a water pump. The lowest pumping power of the water pump was selected for the experiment. The flow rate was adjusted with the help of a flow control valve and a bypass valve. As seen from the figure, the bypass valve diverts the flow back to the pump inlet. The flow meter is placed after the flow control valve. The pipe length before and after the flowmeter was higher than 10 times the diameter of the pipe to perform accurate flow rate measurement and avoid turbulence. The temperature of the flow was measured using a T-type thermocouple after the flow meter and just before the PV/T inlet. The fluid outlet temperature was also measured at the PV/T outlet. The inlet temperature was maintained constant using a water bath. The fluid exiting the PV/T was allowed to pass through a heat exchanger to dissipate the heat to the cold fluid entering the heat exchanger from the water bath. The cooled fluid is then again circulated to the pump and allowed to circulate through the PV/T in a closed loop. Also, thermocouples were inserted to measure the inlet and outlet temperature of the working fluid from the heat exchanger.

Performance investigation of PV/T was conducted at different operating conditions, in the range specified in Table 2 . The system was allowed to reach a steady state at a specific operating condition before the readings were recorded. A comparison was then performed with the performance of three NFs and the base fluids at the same operating conditions. The PV/T inlet fluid temperature was maintained uniformly throughout the experiment by adjusting the coolant fluid temperature at the water bath. The experiments were usually started with the fluid flow rate at 0.2 lpm. The inlet temperature was allowed to reach the desired temperature. A steady state would be achieved after maintaining a constant inlet and outlet temperature for a short period with negligible variation. All the required measurements were recorded at steady-state conditions. Three different radiation intensities of 350, 500 and 650 W m-2, were used in this study. Inlet temperature and flow rate were also varied in the range of 15–30 °C, and 0.2 to 2.5 lpm, respectively.

A second law analysis was conducted on the N-PVT system to account for the irreversibilities occurring in the system. Exergy is the maximum theoretical energy of the system that could be utilised as useful work [37]. Exergetic performance of the system was evaluated after calculating the maximum available thermal and electrical energy that could be produced by the system from the maximum available incident radiation.

Assumptions used for the exergy analysis are provided below [31]:
1.
Exergy destruction due to frictional and pressure drop was neglected
2.
Kinetic and potential energy flows were neglected as they were negligible
3.
Chemical and nuclear interactions were also neglected
4.
Energy inflow and efflux were taken as positive and negative, respectively

The exergy balance equation for the N-PV/T system was formulated and shown in Equations (6)–(14). Exergy due to fluid flowing in and out of the system was calculated using Equations (9) and (10), respectively. Maximum available energy from the sun was calculated using the exergy efficiency model suggested by Petela [38]. The formulae for calculating the exergy from incident solar radiation are provided in Equation (11). The net thermal exergy gain in the fluid due to fluid flow was coined as shown in Equation (12). The thermal and electrical exergy efficiency of the system is the fraction of available thermal and electrical exergy retained from the maximum available solar energy received by the system. Thermal, electrical and overall efficiencies of the N-PV/T were calculated using Equations (13) and (14), and (15), respectively.

Hottel-Whillier-Bliss analysis is a modified method of reducing the instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat plate solar collector for effective comparison of the system based on its design, working fluid and operating parameters. The thermal efficiency of the system is represented in the HWB curve as a function of reduced temperature (
T red
) as shown in Equation (17). The HWB analysis is performed to calculate the heat removal factor (
F r
), overall heat loss coefficient (
U l
), and transmittance-absorbance product (τα) of the collector. The slope of the curve indicates the energy removal parameter (
F r
U l
), which corresponds to the amount of heat lost to the ambient from the PV/T surface. The intersection of the efficiency curve on the Y-axis indicates the energy absorption parameter (
F r
(τα)). This parameter specifies the maximum amount of energy that could be absorbed by the system when the PV/T surface temperature is equal to the ambient temperature. The useful heat gain and instantaneous thermal efficiency based on HWB analysis are provided in Equation (19) and (20), respectively.

The PV/T system's overall energy characteristics data from the experimental study was utilised to evaluate the environmental and economic advantages of employing NFs in PV/T system. The enviro-economic analysis of the current system with base fluid, and optimized NFs (0.15 wt% C-dot, 0.1 wt% MXene, and 0.15 wt% hybrid), was conducted using the methodology developed by Vahidinia et al. [39]. The PV/T system was evaluated by assuming the studied irradiation levels (350, 500, and 650 W m-2) to be the average intensity throughout the day. The flow rate was varied in the range of 0.2–2.5 lpm. Table 3 displays the parameter values that were used in the present calculations. The diurnal carbon dioxide emission (
x

O 2
) from the PV/T operation was determined using Equation (21). The quantity was calculated by multiplying the emission intensity of PV/T (
y

t working
) and the incident solar energy gain (
Q sol
) throughout the day. As the thermal efficiency of NF based system is higher than base fluid, the equivalent required solar energy for the PV/T is lower compared to the base fluid -based system. The solar energy gained from the PV/T aperture area with base fluid and NFs was calculated using Equation (22) and (23), respectively. The emission cost associated with the carbon emission for each operating condition was calculated using the daily carbon emission value, and energy-based carbon emission cost for the PV/T (
c

), as shown in Equation (24) [24]. Based on information found in the literature, the price of carbon emissions and the PV/T emission rate were chosen [40–42].

The error involved in the experimental measurement needs to be accounted for to improve the accuracy of the analysis. Uncertainty analysis helps to quantify the inaccuracy in measurement based on the accuracy of the measurement tools [43]. The Equation (25) describes the general formula for calculating the uncertainty associated with a dependent parameter
y
, which is a function of
x 1
,
x 2
,
x 3
, and
x 4
. Inaccuracy in the calculation of thermal, electrical, and overall energy and exergy efficiencies of the system was evaluated using this general formula. The observations from the uncertainty analysis are provided in Table 4 . The analysis proves that the uncertainty involved with the calculations falls within 2.4 % for all the parameters.

The thermal efficiency of a water-based PV/T system was plotted against the flow rate for three different solar radiation intensities as shown in Fig. 8 (a) to (c). The highest thermal efficiency (48 %) was observed for PV/T when the irradiation was minimum (350 W m-2). As observed from the figure, thermal efficiency decreased with a decrease in the flow rate. Even though the efficiency decreases with a decrease in flow rate, the fluid outlet temperature keeps increasing as the fluid receives more time for the heat transfer process. The lowest thermal efficiency (30.95 %) and highest fluid temperature difference (22.3 °C) were recorded at the lowest flow rate (0.2 lpm). A steep decline in efficiency was observed for flow rates less than 1 lpm, however, the rest of the graph had minimum variation.

The thermal efficiency of C-dot-based PV/T is plotted as shown in Fig. 9 (a)–(c). Fig. 9 (a) depicts the flow rate-dependent thermal efficiency variation at irradiation of 350 W m-2. The graph shows a steady rise in efficiency with flow rate. In comparison to water, the thermal efficiency of the C-dot-based system showed a steady rise in a graph with flow rate. Fig. 9 (b) depicts the thermal efficiency of C-dot-based PV/T at irradiation of 500 W m-2. In comparison to the efficiency curve at 650 W m-2, the thermal efficiency increased steeply from 0.2 to 0.5 lpm. This is because, at the lowest flow rate (0.2 lpm), the heat loss from the system was maximum. This heat loss gets reduced with an increase in flow rate, resulting in a steep rise in thermal efficiency. The lowest thermal efficiency recorded was about 34.7 % at 0.2 lpm and 650 W m-2, as observed in Fig. 9 (c). The highest thermal efficiency and fluid temperature difference of 54 % and 27.87 °C, respectively, was observed at the highest flow rate of 2.5 lpm. Thermal efficiency increased by 6.64 % in comparison to water-based PV/T systems. However, the rise in temperature difference by C-dot was only about 0.6 °C.

MXene-based NFs were found to exhibit almost the same performance as C-dot. The thermal efficiency curve can be observed in Fig. 10 . Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the systems’ thermal efficiency variation with a flow rate of 650 W m-2. The thermal efficiency of the system was found to be higher for lower inlet temperatures. The highest thermal efficiency of 51 % was observed at this irradiation and 2.5 lpm. As shown in Fig. 10 (b), thermal efficiency increased steeply while increasing the flow rate from 0.2 to 0.5 lpm, and later maintained a steady rise with the flow rate. The same observation was made for C-dot NF. Variation of thermal efficiency with a flow rate of 350 W m-2 is shown in Fig. 10 (c). The highest efficiency achieved by PV/T was 52.16 %. And the outlet temperature reached a maximum value of 55.2 °C at the lowest flow rate, and at an irradiation of 650 W m-2.

The thermal efficiency curve of the system with MXene/C-dot NF at various irradiations is shown in Fig. 11 (a) – (c). The thermal efficiency of the system at irradiation of 650 W m-2 is shown in Fig. 11 (a). As observed for other HTFs, the thermal efficiency of the system increased with an increase in fluid flow rate. The system exhibited higher thermal efficiency with lower fluid inlet temperature, due to a higher temperature gradient achieved between mean fluid temperature and PV/T surface temperature. The thermal efficiency of the system at 500 W m-2 is visualized in Fig. 11 (b). The highest thermal efficiency of 57.45 % was achieved by the system at 500 W m-2. Thermal efficiency variation with a flow rate of 350 W m-2 is shown in Fig. 11 (c). At this irradiation level, the highest thermal efficiency of 55.8 % was achieved by the system at a flow rate of 2.5 lpm and inlet temperature of 15 °C. A comparative graph showing the thermal efficiencies of the system with each HTF at different operating conditions is provided in Section S1 in the Supplementary data file.

Variation of electrical efficiency of the PV/T with flow rate for each HTF is shown in Fig. 12 - Fig. 15. The trend observed for each fluid shows a rise in the efficiency curve with flow rate. In the case of DI water, as shown in Fig. 12 (a), the electrical efficiency was found to be a maximum at very low irradiation (350 W m-2) and 2.5 lpm flow rate. The graph shows that the drop in electrical efficiency of PV/T with rise in inlet temperature was minimum when the irradiation was 350 W m-2. The decrease in electrical efficiency was found to increasing as the irradiation intensity increased to 500 W m-2, as shown in Fig. 12 (b). As observed in Fig. 12 (a), electrical efficiency was found to be at a very minimum when the irradiation was maximum (650 W m-2), and the flow rate was minimum (0.2 lpm). The temperature of the PV/T surface is maximum at high irradiation and when the flow rate is not sufficient to cool the PV/T. Similarly, The highest and lowest electrical efficiency produced by the collector was 17.42 % (@350 W m-2, 2.5 lpm) and 16.55 % (@650 W m-2, 0.2 lpm), respectively.

The electrical efficiency graph of PV/T with C-dot, MXene and hybrid NF is visualized in Fig. 13 , Fig. 14 , and Fig. 15 , respectively. The highest electrical efficiency for the PV/T with NFs was recorded at 350 W m-2 and 2.5 lpm. C-dot, MXene and hybrid N-PV/T systems were found to be having electrical efficiency of 17.83, 17.77, and 17.98 %, respectively. The peak power produced by the HTFs at the highest irradiation was 174.96 (DI), 176.84 (C-dot), 176.25 (MXene), and 177.51 W (hybrid). A comparative graph showing the thermal efficiencies of the system with each HTF at different operating conditions is provided in Section S2 in the Supplementary data file.

The IV characteristics and the power curve of the PV/T with different working fluids are shown in Figs. 16 and 17
, respectively. The IV-curve of the system operating at 15 °C inlet temperature and 2.5 lpm was selected at all three irradiation levels to perform the electrical performance comparison of the system with different fluids. In the IV characteristic curve of PV/T at 350 W m-2 as depicted in Fig. 16 (a), the open circuit voltage (Voc) was observed to be enhanced with NFs. The Voc of the system was around 38.5 V with DI water, which was the lowest. Hybrid NF produced the highest enhancement of Voc by about 0.7 V. The IV curve of MXene and C-dot was found to be coinciding with the characteristic curve. The peak voltage and current at the maximum power point have been highlighted in the figure. The corresponding power curve of the system at these operating conditions is shown in Fig. 17 (a). The graph states that the maximum power produced by the system was increased with the application of NFs. The highest power produced by hybrid, C-dot and MXene NFs are 98.09, 97.59, and 97.05 W, respectively. The highest enhancement of around 2.73 W was produced by a hybrid NF over the base fluid. The IV and power characteristics curve of PV/T at an irradiance of 500 W m-2 is visualized in Figs. 16 (b) and Fig. 17 (b), respectively. It could be inferred that the Voc of the system was enhanced with the NFs. Hybrid NF-based PV/T recorded the highest Voc of 33.952 V when compared to 38.9 V with DI water. Correspondingly, Fig. 17 (b) reveals that the power produced by the hybrid NF was about 148.67 W, which was an enhancement of 3W. Figs. 16 (c) and Fig. 17 (c) depict the IV and corresponding power curves of the PV/T with different HTF at irradiation of 650 W m-2. A similar trend as that for curves at lower solar radiation intensity was also observed in this case. However, the Voc was marginally increased with NFs at this irradiation level. Maximum enhancement in power production by around 2.55 W was again recorded for hybrid NF in comparison to DI water.

The average overall energy efficiency of the system was calculated as shown in Fig. 18 . The graph reveals that the overall efficiency of PV/T with hybrid was higher than other HTFs except at very low flow rates (<0.5 lpm). Hybrid-based PV/T recorded an average overall efficiency of 71.5 % at 2.5 lpm. The overall efficiency of PV/T with C-dot was better than other fluids below 0.5 lpm and maintained a significant enhancement over DI water. The efficiency curve of MXene produced a similar trend as that of C-dot. The average overall efficiencies of C-dot and MXene were 68.4 and 68.1 %, respectively at a flow rate of 2.5 lpm.

The average thermal exergy efficiency of the system at each flow rate for different irradiation and inlet temperatures was calculated and depicted as shown in Fig. 19 . Equation (12) and (16) state that the thermal exergy efficiency is highly dependent on the fluid outlet temperature, temperature difference, flow rate, and irradiation. Thermal exergy efficiency was observed to be diminishing with a rise in flow rate. This is due to the decrease in fluid outlet temperature and temperature difference with an increase in flow rate. Also, the experiment with each fluid begins at 0.2 lpm when the ambient temperature is low. As the experiment progressed by increasing the flow rate, the ambient temperature also increased. Even though the ambient temperature is kept almost constant for each fluid, there is variation in ambient temperature at different flow rates. Hence, the highest thermal exergy recorded for C-dot, hybrid, and MXene-based PV/T systems were 3.06, 2.91, and 2.85 %, respectively. PV/T with C-dot NF exhibited higher thermal exergy performance at low flow rates (<0.5 lpm), while hybrid NF performed better at moderate flow rates (0.5, 1 and 1.5 lpm). However, the thermal exergy efficiency of DI water was observed to be higher than the NFs at flow rates above 1.5 lpm.

An electrical exergy efficiency comparison was performed with all the working fluids as shown in Fig. 20 . The exergy efficiency of the fluids was found to be less than the energy efficiency of the maximum available solar energy, as calculated in Equation (11) , which was used for the analysis. In contrast to thermal exergy efficiency, the average electrical exergy efficiency of all the fluids increased with an increase in flow rate. A steep gradient was observed till 0.5lpm. From then onwards, the exergy efficiency increased steadily. The hybrid NF exhibited the highest electrical exergy efficiency in comparison to its counterparts. The highest electrical exergy efficiency achieved by hybrid, C-dot, and MXene NFs was 18.44, 18.33, and 18.24 %, respectively. The NFs generated an enhancement in the electrical exergy efficiency of PV/T over water which was about 0.18–0.38 %.

The overall exergy of the PV/T system with different working fluids is visualized in Fig. 21 . The overall exergy efficiency was observed to be highly dominated by the electrical efficiency of the system. However, as the variation in electrical exergy efficiency with flow rate is minimal compared to thermal exergy, the variation in overall efficiency with flow rate was highly influenced by thermal exergy. Hence, the trend in the overall exergy curve is almost like that of thermal exergy efficiency. Hybrid NF was found to have higher overall efficiency across the flow rates, except at 0.5 lpm. The highest exergy efficiency achieved by hybrid NF was about 20.68 %, which was recorded at 0.5 lpm during which an enhancement of 0.67 % was achieved over water.

The surface temperature profile of PV/T was measured for each NF and DI water at the same operating conditions of 600 W m-2, 2.5 lpm, and 20 °C inlet temperature. The temperature was recorded using an infrared temperature gun after the system had achieved a steady state. As observed from Fig. 22 , the surface temperature of PV/T with DI water was found to be higher than that with other fluids. The temperature variation range is provided along with each contour plot. It could be inferred that the temperature variation is similar for each fluid, but the intensity varies across the surface. The fluid enters from the top-right corner of the figure and the exit is through the lower-left corner. As the fluid temperature is lowest at the inlet, the thermal gradient was higher thereby maximising the heat transfer from the PV/T surface to the fluid. Also, the clearance between the riser tube and the edge is larger at the lower portion and the thermal absorber sheet was pierced at the lowermost portion to accommodate the junction box. This has led to an increase in surface temperature at the lowermost part when compared to other regions. The non-uniformity in simulated irradiation was the major reason for the lower temperature in the middle section in comparison to the upper region (inlet section). The solar irradiation distribution over the PV/T surface, shown in Fig. 6, confirms the variation. The highest temperature at the PV/T surface was observed to be 61.2 °C with DI water. In the case of hybrid NF, the highest temperature observed on the PV/T surface was 53 °C, which was considerably lower than MXene (59.5 °C) and C-dot (58.6 °C) NFs. The average surface temperature of the PV/T was around 44.2, 45.2, and 46.4 °C with hybrid, C-dot, and MXene, respectively. The reduction in surface temperature with NF was about 3.4, 2.4, and 1.3 °C, respectively.

The variation of thermal efficiency of the PV/T system with various working fluids is shown in Fig. 23 . The HWB analysis curve was plotted with all the experimental data points from different irradiation conditions (350, 500, and 650 W m-2). The intention was to plot a curve that fits the system efficiency at all extreme operating conditions. Numerous ways to plot the HWB curve were initially considered by categorizing the data points based on various operating conditions (radiation intensity, fluid outlet temperature, etc.). However, as each categorization would yield a different heat removal factor and zero-loss efficiency, the method was dropped. Also, the points are observed to be scattered because, at the same irradiation, the reduced temperature data point value would be the same for two different operating conditions. The reduced temperature value at two extreme operating conditions (e.g.: (1) low inlet temperature and low flow rate, and (2) high inlet temperature and high flow rate) was found to be the same. Even though the reduced temperature is the same for both data points the thermal efficiency of the system is different because the flow rate is different. Hence it is difficult to select specific data points for plotting the curve and cannot be considered as a comprehensive method of analyzing the thermal efficiency of the system. HWB analysis was added just to supplement the energy and exergy analysis that was already discussed in the previous sections. The intercept and slope of the curve for each working fluid correspond to the absorbed energy parameter (Fr. (τα)) and removal energy parameter (Fr.Ul) of the system, respectively. The values of each parameter for the fluids as obtained from the graph are provided in Table 5 . From Fig. 23 (a), the heat removal factor and overall heat loss coefficient of the water-based PV/T were calculated to be 0.428 and 2.456 Wm−2K−1, respectively. Fig. 23 (b), (c) and (d) depicts the efficiency curves of the system with C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NF. As inferred from the curves, the absorbed energy factors were found to be ameliorated with the application of NFs. The value increased from 0.428 for DI to 0.462, 0.451, and 0.482 with C-dot, MXene and hybrid NF, respectively. It was also noticed that the removal energy parameter, Fr.Ul, increased from 2.456 for DI water to 2.805, 2.843 and 3.870 for C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs, respectively. Hence, the energy absorption and energy removal parameter was influenced by the fluid thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, and fluid temperature gradient across the system. NF with better thermophysical properties increases the energy absorbed by the system for the same operating conditions. A comparison of efficiency curves of the system with different working fluids is depicted in Fig. 24 . As observed, the thermal efficiency of the system was higher with NFs when compared with that of DI water. The intersection of the efficiency curves of PV/T with each fluid shows that at specific operating conditions, different fluids would be performing better. It could be observed that the efficiency of PV/T with C-dot NF was higher compared to other fluids as reduced temperature increases from 0.0184 Km2W-1. The fitted line of the hybrid NF intersects with MXene at a reduced temperature of 0.0299. This shows that MXene and C-dot-based PV/T have better thermal performance compared to hybrid and DI water.

The effect of solar irradiation and flow rate on the enviro-economic parameters are depicted as shown in Figs. 25 and 26
. Variations of carbon dioxide emission quantity from the PV/T operating on water, C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NF are shown in Fig. 25 (a), (b), (c), and (d), respectively. The study reveals that for water, the emission is almost constant for varying flow rates. However, carbon emission increased steeply by around 1.04 kg with a rise in irradiation from 350 to 650 W m-2. As observed in Fig. 25 (b), (c) and (d), the variation in carbon dioxide emission of PV/T with all the NFs was non-linear when compared to that of the base fluid. This variation is because an enhancement in overall energy efficiency with NF reduces the required solar energy gain equivalent to that of the base fluid. Equation (22) and (23) depict the reason for the above-mentioned observation. Also, the emission was found to be decreasing with an increase in flow rate. The lowest emission value for NFs was calculated to be at an average daily irradiation of 350 W m-2 and a maximum flow rate of 2.5 lpm. The maximum CO2 emission from PV/T with C-dot, MXene, and hybrid NFs, were calculated to be around 2.024, 1.979, and 1.931 kgCO2/day, respectively. The results show that the irradiation is the predominant factor that influences the carbon emission. PV/T with hybrid NF exhibited the lowest emission rate of 1.152 kgCO2/day, at 350 W m-2. The main reason is attributed to the highest thermal efficiency of PV/T with a hybrid NF.

The corresponding emission cost for PV/T depicted in Fig. 26, clearly depicts that the variation in the cost of environmental pollution is similar to the variation of emission rate as observed in Fig. 25. This could be observed from the fact that the emission cost is linearly dependent on the emission rate as depicted in Equation (24). The results show that the emission cost of PV/T with a hybrid NF-based system is lower than that of other HTFs. The carbon emission price for HTFs at the highest irradiation level of 650 W m-2 are 0.152 (DI water), 0.137 (C-dot), 0.134 (MXene), and 0.131 $/day (hybrid). A hybrid NF-based system was found to reduce the daily emission cost by 0.021 $/day over a water-based system.

The average reduction in emission rate and cost associated with each type of HTF-based system at different radiation intensities is shown in Fig. 27 . The data shows that the adoption of NFs reduces the emission rate in the range 0.02–0.3 kgCO2/day. The highest emission rate reduction (0.309 kgCO2/day) was achieved with a hybrid NF at 650 W m-2. The graph shows that emission reduction is highest for hybrid NF, followed by C-dot and MXene NF, at all radiation levels. Emission cost reduction shown in Fig. 27 states that the hybrid NF achieved the highest reduction of 0.02 $/day which was a considerable decrement of 13.5 % over the water-based PV/T system. C-dot and MXene NF exhibited a similar reduction in emission cost by about 11.66 and 9.64 %, respectively.

In this research, water-based C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs were developed for application as HTF in PV/T systems. The concentration of the NFs was optimized based on thermo-economic parameters. To examine the thermal and electrical performance enhancement over DI water, the optimized NFs were experimentally tested in PV/T systems. The system was evaluated based on energy and exergy perspectives. HWB and enviro-economic analysis was also carried out on the nanofluid-based PV/T system. This study was the first to undertake a comprehensive investigation that incorporated nanofluid concentration optimization, experimental investigation on PV/T, and a detailed analysis (energy, energy, HWB, and enviro-economic analysis). The major findings from the study are:
•
Energy analysis shows that a thermal efficiency of 57.45 % was achieved with the hybrid NF (at operating conditions of 500 W m-2, 2.5 lpm, and 15 °C), which produced an enhancement of 9.45 % over DI water. The electrical efficiency of the PV/T was found to be slightly influenced by NFs. Hybrid NFs recorded an electrical efficiency of 17.98 % and generated a peak power of 177.51 W (at 350 W m-2, 2.5 lpm, and 15 °C), which produced an enhancement of 0.56 % and 2.6 W, respectively, over base fluid.
•
The highest overall energy efficiency recorded for PV/T with DI, C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs were 65.61, 71.13, 69.93, and 75.36 %, respectively.
•
Exergy analysis shows that thermal exergy efficiency decreases with flow rate. C-dot, hybrid and DI water exhibited higher thermal exergy efficiency at low (0.2, 0.3 lpm), moderate (0.5–1.5 lpm) and high flow rates (2–2.5 lpm), respectively. Electrical exergy efficiency was observed to increase with flow rate. Hybrid NF-based PV/T performed better in comparison to other fluids.
•
The overall exergy efficiency of hybrid NF-based PV/T was higher across the flow rates. The highest exergy efficiency of 20.68 % was recorded by hybrid, which produced an enhancement of 0.67 % over DI.
•
Hottel-Whillier-Bliss analysis shows that hybrid NF-based PV/T could achieve higher thermal efficiency when the inlet temperature is close to the ambient. An enhancement in thermal efficiency by about 5.4 % was achieved with the hybrid. However, the heat loss coefficient of the PV/T with DI was lower than that of NFs.
•
Enviro-economic analysis shows that PV/T with hybrid NF showed the lowest daily carbon emission rate (1.152 kgCO2/day at 350 W m-2). In comparison to DI water, the highest emission reduction of about 0.309 kgCO2/day was achieved with hybrid NF. The carbon emission price for HTFs at the highest irradiation level of 650 W m-2 were 0.152 (DI water), 0.137 (C-dot), 0.134 (MXene), and 0.131 $/day (hybrid). Hybrid NF-based PV/T was found to achieve 13.5 % reduction in carbon emission price over DI water. A comprehensive life-cycle assessment of the system would be conducted in the future studies.

Overall, the study states that all the NFs enhance the heat transfer efficiency and thereby thermal, electrical, and enviro-economic performance of PV/T system, in comparison to DI water. Among the HTFs, hybrid MXene/C-dot NF performed better in most of the operating conditions. Future research would focus on developing an experimentally validated numerical model for predicting the performance at various operating conditions.

Sreehari Sreekumar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Adrian Pugsley: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Neil Hewitt: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
